questions.question: In the MWPM decoding graph, what do the vertices represent?
questions.explanation: Vertices in the matching graph represent syndrome defects—locations where the syndrome has changed from the expected value. These correspond to the endpoints of error chains.

questions.question: Why do errors in surface codes create pairs of defects?

options.text: Because an error anticommutes with exactly two adjacent stabilizers

questions.explanation: A single Pauli error on a surface code qubit anticommutes with exactly two adjacent stabilizers, causing both to give -1 outcomes. This creates a pair of defects that can be matched.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the basic Blossom algorithm for V vertices?
questions.explanation: Edmonds' Blossom algorithm has O(V³) worst-case time complexity. However, optimized implementations and sparse graph structures often achieve better performance in practice.

questions.question: How do measurement errors appear in the 3D matching graph?

options.text: As edges connecting different spatial locations at the same time
As edges connecting the same spatial location at different times
They don't appear in the matching graph

questions.explanation: Measurement errors create temporal defect pairs—a syndrome that appears wrong in one round but correct before and after. In the 3D graph, this appears as an edge connecting the same location at different times.

questions.question: What do boundary nodes in the matching graph represent?

options.text: Qubits at the edge of the code
The possibility that an error chain connects a defect to the boundary

questions.explanation: Boundary nodes represent the possibility that an error chain connects a single defect to the code boundary. This is necessary because errors can create a single visible defect if the chain ends at the boundary.

questions.question: What is the approximate threshold of MWPM decoding for surface codes?
questions.explanation: MWPM achieves a threshold of approximately 1% for surface codes under phenomenological noise models. This is close to the optimal threshold of about 1.1%.

questions.question: In MWPM decoding, edge weights typically represent the number of physical errors (or negative log probability) along the corresponding error chain.
questions.explanation: True. Edge weights represent the 'cost' of an error chain, which can be the number of single-qubit errors or the negative log probability. MWPM finds the matching that minimizes total weight, corresponding to the most probable error configuration.
